text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 2 August 2024 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

Alex Turner, DOB: 12-11-1992, Hospital No: U428517 NHS No. 1045827936  
22 Brookside Close, London, N7 8PA 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy following adolescent onset/remote history of mild head injury without intracranial bleed/migraine without aura/anxiety symptoms under primary care review/no known drug allergies/non-smoker/rare alcohol use. 

Medication: Sodium valproate 500 mg in the morning and 1000 mg at night. 
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily. 
Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily, Omeprazole 20 mg daily, Propranolol 40 mg as needed for migraine prophylaxis (patient-reported limited use).  

I reviewed Alex in clinic today, attended alone. He reports generally stable wellbeing and good adherence to antiepileptic therapy, with pillbox use and a phone reminder app. Sleep has been irregular during the recent heatwave ï¿½ he estimates 5–6 hours most nights. Appetite is normal. Weight is stable. No recent intercurrent illnesses. He continues to work part-time in retail and has avoided lone high-risk tasks per workplace adjustments. 

Seizures: The patient has generalised epilepsy. He brought a handwritten seizure diary and a smartphone calendar screenshot. Seizure events on 07-03, 07-07, 07-10, 07-18 were documented, each described as brief generalised tonic–clonic episodes lasting under 2 minutes, with post-ictal fatigue for several hours and no tongue biting reported on two of the dates. No injuries or emergency attendances. Triggers likely include sleep deprivation and heat; he also missed an evening dose on 07-07. There were no absence spells or myoclonic clusters reported outside these dates. Prior to July, he had been seizure-free since late March. 

Examination: Alert and oriented; speech fluent. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Motor power full in all limbs, tone normal; no focal deficit. Gait steady. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. No rash. Observed mild bilateral postural tremor, long-standing per patient. 

Investigations: Last U&E/LFTs in May were within reference ranges; valproate level in May mid-therapeutic. No recent lamotrigine level. MRI brain (2021) structurally normal. EEG (2022) showed generalised spike-and-wave discharges consistent with a generalised epilepsy syndrome. 

Management discussion: We reviewed adherence, sleep hygiene, and hydration in hot weather. He understands driving restrictions and confirms he does not drive. Rescue plan reiterated for any seizure >5 minutes or seizure clusters Ã‚Â· family aware. He prefers to avoid medication change today, given the clear potential triggers in July and absence of injuries. He agreed to increase sleep regularity and avoid missed doses; he will use two alarms â€ and a weekly blister pack. He declined a switch to once-daily modified-release valproate for now. 

Plan: 
- Continue sodium valproate 500 mg AM and 1000 mg PM; continue lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily. 
- Order repeat LFTs, FBC, and valproate level in 2 weeks; add lamotrigine level to assess adherence/clearance in heat. 
- Provide written seizure action plan; reinforce hydration, sleep schedule, and sick-day rules. 
- If further generalised seizures occur despite optimised sleep and adherence in the next 6–8 weeks, consider valproate MR conversion or lamotrigine uptitration (shared decision at follow-up). 
- Epilepsy specialist nurse phone check-in in 3 weeks; routine clinic review in 3 months or sooner if breakthrough events recur. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter details specific seizure events: 'Seizure events on 07-03, 07-07, 07-10, 07-18 were documented,' which are four events within July. It also states, 'Prior to July, he had been seizure-free since late March.' Therefore, the current measurable frequency from the most recent period is 4 seizures in one month (July). No other overlapping frequencies are provided that would be higher. Thus, normalize to '4 per month.'
